beats.text: You read and created bar graphs with scale of 10. You figured out values at the lines AND between the lines. Let's look at some patterns you discovered.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong graph on first attempt

steps.text: Look closely. Count the lines on each graph. Which one has the fewest?

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong graph on second attempt

steps.text: Let me help. The graph on the right shows only 2 lines: 0, 10, 20. Count the lines on the others. Which has the fewest?

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong graph three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Scale of 10 graph highlights to show the correct answer.

steps.text: Let me show you. The scale of 1 graph has 20 lines. The scale of 2 has 10 lines. The scale of 5 has 4 lines. The scale of 10 has only 2 lines. Scale of 10 uses the fewest lines because each line represents a bigger jump.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Look for the bar that reaches between the 30 and 40 lines.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong category on second attempt

params.description: Category B bar highlights in vertical graph.

steps.text: Here's a hint: follow the gridlines to see where each bar stops. The bar showing 35 is between 30 and 40. Which category is that?

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong category three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Guideline draws from top of Category B bar to axis, showing it lands between 30 and 40.

steps.text: Let me show you. I follow the top of each bar to see where it lands on the axis. Category A reaches 20. Category C reaches 40. Category D reaches 15. Category B reaches between 30 and 40, so that's 35. Category B is the answer.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

steps.text: Look at where the bar ends. Which bar reaches between the 30 and 40 lines?

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong category on second attempt

params.description: Category B bar highlights in horizontal graph.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. In a horizontal graph, bars go sideways instead of up. Find the bar that reaches between 30 and 40. Which one is it?

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong category three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Guideline draws from end of Category B bar to axis, showing it lands between 30 and 40.

steps.text: This one takes a bit of thought, so let's work through it together. I check where each bar ends. Category A ends at 20. Category C ends at 40. Category D ends at 15. Category B ends between 30 and 40, which is 35. The bars go sideways, but the axis still shows the same values. Category B is the answer.

incorrects.description: Branch 2: Student selected only A (under-selecting, correct but incomplete)

params.description: Image A pulses to confirm selection.

steps.text: Money does use 10s. Look at the other images. Are there any others that group things by 10?

incorrects.description: Branch 2: Student selected only B (under-selecting, correct but incomplete)

params.description: Image B pulses to confirm selection.

steps.text: Measuring does use 10s. Look at the other images. Are there any others that group things by 10?

incorrects.description: Branch 3: Student selected only C or nothing (all-wrong)

params.description: Images A and B dim slightly to draw attention.

steps.text: Scale of 10 means counting in groups of 10. Look at the money and the ruler. What pattern do you see on both?

incorrects.description: Branch 4: Student selected C plus at least one correct answer (mixed correct and incorrect)

params.description: Images A and B highlight, C dims.

steps.text: You found situations that use 10s. But counting one by one doesn't use groups of 10. Which images show counting by 10s?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Images A and B highlight. Annotation appears: '10, 20, 30...' over both.

steps.text: Let me show you. Look at image A: dollar bills in stacks of 10. Look at image B: the ruler has marks at 10, 20, 30. Both count by 10s. Image C counts one by one, not in groups of 10. The answers are A and B. When you see bigger numbers organized in groups of 10, scale of 10 makes counting faster.

condition.selected: I picked the line closest to the top of the bar

incorrects.description: Student selected the closest-line strategy (doesn't account for between-line precision)

params.description: Bar remains highlighted. Ghost gridlines at 5s pulse briefly.

steps.text: That gets you close, but the bar is exactly halfway between 40 and 50. What number is halfway?

condition.selected: I added the two numbers on the lines

incorrects.description: Student selected adding the gridline numbers (arithmetic error—confuses sum with midpoint)

params.description: Bar remains highlighted. Ghost gridlines at 5s pulse briefly.

steps.text: Adding 40 and 50 gives 90, which is too big. To find halfway, you need half of the difference. What's half of 10?

incorrects.description: Student selected guessing (lacks awareness of the halfway pattern strategy)

params.description: Bar remains highlighted. Ghost gridlines at 5s pulse briefly.

steps.text: You don't need to guess. There's a pattern: halfway between any two lines is always 5 more than the lower line. Why is that?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct strategy.

params.description: Ghost gridlines at 5s pulse. The space between 40 and 50 highlights briefly, then the halfway point at 45 glows.

steps.text: Let me show you the pattern. The bar is halfway between 40 and 50. The lines are 10 apart. Half of 10 is 5. So halfway is always 5 more than the lower line: 40 plus 5 equals 45. That pattern works for any bar graph with scale of 10. Halfway between the lines is always 5 more.
